title: How is GDP actually measured, and why does the number keep changing?
tension: The number that moves markets, sets the government's borrowing, and wins or loses elections is, in the end, an estimate, one its own makers revise as many as five times, built partly on assumptions and partly on standing in for the unmeasured informal economy. And the last two times India changed how it measures, the politics caught fire: the 2015 method showed faster growth than expected, and the 2018 back-series rewrote the growth record of a previous government. You are trusting a number that the statisticians themselves keep rewriting, which is exactly why it is worth knowing how it is built.
teachingGoal: Pull back the curtain on the machine: who computes India's GDP, why the first estimate is a forecast, why the same quarter is revised repeatedly (and why that is normal, not fraud), what a base year is and why moving it matters, the politically charged back-series episode, and how much of the number is estimated rather than directly measured. The reader should come away trusting the direction of GDP while understanding its fragility.
sectionArc: Who actually computes India's GDP? (MoSPI and the National Statistical Office; the National Accounts Statistics; the release calendar)
Why is the first GDP number really a forecast? (Advance Estimates released before the year is even over; the benchmark-indicator method; data not yet in)
Why does the same quarter get revised again and again? (Advance to Provisional to Revised estimates; late tax, corporate and survey data; why revision is honesty, not manipulation)
What is a base year, and why move it? (rebasing; the 2011-12 base and the pending move; why an old base distorts a changing economy)
Why did rewriting the past growth record cause a political storm? (the 2018 back-series; two versions; the adopted one lowered earlier-government growth; the contest over it)
How much of GDP is estimated rather than measured? (the informal sector; corporate-data proxies; the soft underbelly the critics target)
So how much should you trust a GDP number? (trust the direction, distrust the decimal; revisions are routine, but the estimation choices are real and contested)
mustTeach: GDP is compiled by MoSPI / the National Statistical Office in the National Accounts Statistics
the first estimates (Advance Estimates) are made before the year ends and are later revised
the same period is revised through Provisional and Revised Estimates as fuller data arrives; revision is normal
a base year fixes the prices used for real GDP and is periodically updated (rebasing)
the 2018 back-series produced competing versions and became politically contested
a large share of activity is informal and estimated via proxies, which is the main source of measurement doubt

contextCards.fact: India's latest-year GDP figures are Advance Estimates, made before the financial year is complete using indicators like the index of industrial production, GST collections, corporate results and crop estimates, then revised several times (Provisional, then First, Second and Third Revised Estimates) as fuller data arrives, before becoming final. Revision is built into the system, not a sign of error.
contextCards.source: MoSPI National Accounts release practice (standard, uncontested)

contextCards.fact: Until 2015 India headlined 'GDP at factor cost'. In the January 2015 base-year revision (base year moved from 2004-05 to 2011-12), the National Statistical Office switched the headline to Gross Value Added at basic prices and GDP at market prices, aligning with the United Nations System of National Accounts. The new series also showed faster growth for some overlapping years than the old one, which surprised economists and fed a debate about whether post-2011 growth was overstated.
contextCards.source: MoSPI / NSO base-year revision, January 2015; widely reported debate; Subramanian et al., PIIE

contextCards.fact: In 2018 India released a 'back-series' re-estimating growth before 2011 on the new method. Two versions appeared: a National Statistical Commission committee's version (July 2018) showed faster growth in the 2004-2014 years of the previous UPA government, while the official version adopted in November 2018 showed slower growth for those years. The downward revision of the earlier government's record, and the role of the government think-tank NITI Aayog in presenting it, was attacked by the opposition (the former finance minister called it a 'hatchet job') and questioned by some former statisticians, while the government defended the new methodology. The episode hardened public suspicion of GDP data.
contextCards.source: 2018 back-series controversy; CSO (Nov 2018) vs NSC committee (July 2018); contemporaneous reporting (Business Today, Business Standard)

contextCards.fact: Economists including Arvind Subramanian and colleagues at the Peterson Institute for International Economics have argued the post-2011 method overestimates growth, partly because it leans on formal-sector corporate data to stand in for the much larger informal sector, which was hit hard after 2015 by demonetisation, GST and COVID. India revised its GDP methodology again in February 2026, partly to address such concerns. The argument is live, not settled, and the NSO has defended its approach.
contextCards.source: Anand, Felman & Subramanian, PIIE Working Paper 26-3 (March 2026); Subramanian, PIIE (2019); MoSPI methodology revision, February 2026
